sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 54-year-old male, presents with a chronic history of stabbing, shooting pain in the right cheekbone area of his face, which has been ongoing for approximately two to three months.
He reports that the pain is exacerbated by temperature changes, cold weather, and wind.
The pain is described as excruciating and can last from a few minutes to an hour.
The patient denies any sensory loss, numbness, or tingling in the affected area and reports no weakness on the right side of his face.
He has tried ibuprofen, Motrin, and tiger balm with no relief.
He has been referred to a neurosurgeon by his family doctor.
He denies a history of multiple sclerosis, brain tumors, or migraines.
Past medical history:
The patient has occasional headaches, which he treats with Tylenol, but this is different from the facial pain he is experiencing.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any symptoms in other organs or systems relevant to his initial presentation.
Current medications:
He takes Tylenol for occasional headaches.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
The patient follows my finger in both directions and shows symmetric teeth.
He can hear a cotton ball on both sides of his face.
The patient reports a little numbness on the right side of his face, but not on the left.
When poked with a needle, the right side feels dull, while the left side feels sharp.
Test Results:
The patient's MRI shows a small blood vessel abutting and potentially pinching the trigeminal nerve.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has trigeminal neuralgia, a condition characterized by shooting electrical pain in the face due to nerve compression.
Problem list: Trigeminal neuralgia; active
Plan:
I prescribed Tegretol for the patient's trigeminal neuralgia, with a dosage and frequency to be determined.
I will perform genetic testing to ensure the medication is safe for him.
If the pain persists, we will discuss surgical options.
The patient will return in one month to assess the effectiveness of the medication.
Follow-up:
The patient will return in one month to check on the effectiveness of the medication and discuss further treatment options if necessary.
